items.title: Google Security Engineer Arrested in Million-Dollar Polymarket Trading Scheme
items.summary: According to federal prosecutors, Michele Spagnuolo made more than $1 million on the prediction market platform using confidential information about Google Search traffic.

items.title: Huawei's ‘Chip Queen’ Throws Down the Gauntlet
items.summary: The Chinese company is adapting to the demise of Moore’s Law, which guides chip production. It could complicate US chip dominance.

items.title: A Dating App Is Giving Away Free Gas to Convince People to Get Out of the House
items.summary: Amid ongoing economic anxieties, BLK and other companies are giving away basic essentials to appeal to the public.

items.title: Rivian will deliver the first R2 SUVs on June 9
items.summary: The company has finally set a date for the first customer deliveries of what CEO RJ Scaringe has said is "maybe the most important thing we’ve launched to date."

items.title: Triomics nabs $22M to bring oncology-specific AI to cancer centers
items.summary: The Series B round was led by Battery Ventures.

items.title: In more good news for Amazon, Snowflake signs $6B deal with AWS for AI CPU chips
items.summary: Snowflake has signed a new, enormous five-year deal with Amazon to secure chips for AI usage. Nvidia is once again being put on notice.

items.title: Israel issues evacuation order for swathes of southern Lebanon
items.summary: The military says areas south of the Zahrani River are now "combat zones" as it threatens Hezbollah with fresh strikes.

items.title: Ebola-hit DR Congo faces 'catastrophic collision' of disease and conflict, WHO warns
items.summary: Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said fighting in DR Congo was hampering efforts to stop spread

items.title: Trump says US 'not satisfied' with Iran deal yet
items.summary: The US president says he believes Iran wants to reach a deal but there is still no agreement on its terms.

items.title: Traders are skeptical of Iran timeline for Strait of Hormuz reopening
items.summary: Despite reports Iran could return the passageway to normal traffic flows within a month of a peace deal, Kalshi traders thought that was unlikely.

items.title: Jamie Dimon says JPMorgan Chase could spend $20 billion on acquisition: 'We are on the lookout'
items.summary: A $20 billion deal would be among the largest in JPMorgan's history and could invite regulatory scrutiny given its position as the largest U.S. bank by assets.

items.title: Your AI agent can now trade for you on Robinhood. And buy stuff with your credit card too
items.summary: The new products allow customers to create AI assistants capable of carrying out investing strategies or spending instructions with minimal human involvement.

items.title: Stocks Sink in Broad AI Rout Sparked by China's DeepSeek
items.summary: U.S. stocks were mostly lower, with the Nasdaq leading declines as makers of AI infrastructure suffered steep falls, many in the double digits. Nvidia was down 16%.

items.title: Comex Gold, Silver Settle Lower
items.summary: Gold settled 1.4% lower, down two of the past three sessions, and silver fell 2.5%, down three of the past four sessions.

items.title: DeepSeek Won't Sink U.S. AI Titans
items.summary: Panic fueling the selloff of Nvidia, Broadcom and other tech giants is overblown.

items.title: At least $60M from National Park entrance fee fund going toward Trump’s DC projects
items.summary: At least $60 million in National Park Service (NPS) funds are going toward Washington, D.C.-based projects as President Trump seeks to “beautify” the city ahead of the nation’s 250th anniversary, contracts reveal. Projects that aim to improve city fountains and memorials are being funded by NPS funds that are collected through the Recreation Enhancement Fee Program.  This program receives funding from visitor entrance fees. At least…

items.title: Hegseth heads to Asia with Taiwan questions swirling
items.summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth is headed to Asia this week, where he will attend the continent’s biggest defense forum as questions swirl about the U.S.’s support for Taiwan.  Hegseth, who will depart for the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore on Wednesday, will huddle with Singaporean Prime Minister Lawrence Wong and Minister of Defense Chan Chun Sing,…

items.title: US ‘disappointed’ after Netherlands blocks takeover of online ID platform
items.summary: The U.S. said Wednesday that it was “disappointed” in a decision by the Dutch government to block New York-based Kyndryl’s planned acquisition of a cloud services provider over concerns about national security. “We were disappointed by the Dutch government’s decision regarding the Kyndryl-Solvinity deal,” the U.S. Embassy in The Hague, Netherlands, said in a statement.…

items.title: IDVO’s International Dividend Strategy Carries an Unhedged Currency Risk That Can Wipe Out a Year of Yield

items.title: Ecopetrol Launches $492 Million Tender Offer for Brava Energia Stake

items.title: Do Wall Street Analysts Like Corpay Stock?
items.summary: Do Wall Street Analysts Like Corpay Stock?

items.title: Scientists break 30-year superconductivity record at normal pressure
items.summary: Scientists at the University of Houston have shattered a long-standing superconductivity record, creating a material that can conduct electricity with zero resistance at the highest temperature ever achieved under normal pressure conditions. Their breakthrough pushes superconductivity to 151 Kelvin (minus 122°C), beating a record that stood for more than 30 years.

items.title: NASA’s Webb telescope discovers a planet where rock clouds vanish every night
items.summary: A giant planet nearly 700 light-years away has a bizarre daily weather cycle where mineral clouds appear every morning and vanish by nightfall. Using the James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers discovered that WASP-94A b’s mornings are filled with clouds made of rock-like minerals, while its evenings are surprisingly clear. The finding gave scientists their clearest look yet into the planet’s atmosphere and revealed it’s far more Jupiter-like than previously believed.

items.title: NASA’s Fermi telescope reveals the power source behind monster supernovae
items.summary: NASA’s Fermi telescope has detected what may be the first confirmed gamma-ray signal from a superluminous supernova — one of the most extreme explosions in the universe. Scientists believe the blast was powered by a rapidly spinning magnetar, an exotic neutron star with unbelievably strong magnetic fields. The event, called SN 2017egm, erupted 440 million light-years away and may help explain why some supernovae become extraordinarily bright.

items.title: Huge volcanic eruption offers clues to fighting climate change
items.summary: The South Pacific blast may have consumed its own methane — but using this idea against the greenhouse gas is controversial.

items.title: Grapefruit-sized hail may become more common in a warmer world
items.summary: A global model suggests that climate change could make hailstones larger and more damaging in many regions, especially at mid-to-high latitudes.

items.title: AI bots ignore evidence. Can we trust them with science?
items.summary: Scientists rethink their ideas after experiments. AI agents struggle to learn from evidence and recognize when an idea is obviously incorrect.
